speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're focusing on a very practical question: how do you actually access these workshop materials, read them in the format that works best for you, download them, and keep your copy current over time?

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that this is practical, because learners may be wondering, is there one official way to read all this?

speaker: ALEX
text: No, and that's the point. The materials are available in multiple formats. There are Markdown source files, pre-built HTML pages, and, when enabled by the facilitator, a live GitHub Pages website. This appendix exists to explain how to use each option, including offline access and updates.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And we should mention the administrative context too, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. There is an audio companion episode for this material, this appendix is a reference companion to the pre-workshop setup chapter, and the authoritative source for the materials is the Community-Access GitHub repository for Git Going with GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before we walk through the options, here are some quick access tips tailored to different users.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with screen reader users.

speaker: ALEX
text: If you're using a screen reader, the GitHub Pages site includes skip-to-content links and ARIA landmarks. On those pages, you can press D in NVDA or R in JAWS to move to the main landmark. On GitHub.com, if you're on the repository main page, press T to open the file finder so you can type a filename and jump straight to it without working through the full file tree. And after cloning the repository, opening it in VS Code gives you the Explorer panel with Control+Shift+E, where you can browse the tree with arrow keys.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about low vision learners?

speaker: ALEX
text: The HTML version in the html folder usually provides the most polished reading experience. It's styled, navigable, and works offline in a browser. If you're reading Markdown source files, VS Code's built-in preview with Control+Shift+V can render headings, links, and code blocks in a cleaner way than raw text alone. And if you're downloading a ZIP from GitHub, remember that the Code button is the green button near the top of the repository page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for sighted users more generally?

speaker: ALEX
text: There are three main access paths: the GitHub Pages site, which is online and styled; GitHub.com, where Markdown is rendered in the browser; and a local clone, which works offline and is editable. The docs folder holds the Markdown source, the html folder holds pre-built HTML, and cloning with Git gives you a full copy that you can later update with git pull. A ZIP download is just a snapshot and does not update by itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: A simple way to think about the flow is this: you can browse online, read on GitHub.com, download everything, download just one file, learn what each folder contains, read offline, update your local copy, and then decide which format best matches your needs.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So let's take those one at a time. Suppose I just want to browse online in the easiest possible way.

speaker: ALEX
text: If the facilitator has enabled GitHub Pages for the repository, the workshop materials may be available as a website at a GitHub Pages URL, something like an organization dot github dot io address followed by the site name. Your facilitator should provide the exact URL.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And once I have it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open it in your browser. The landing page, usually index dot html, functions as the workshop homepage and is effectively the website counterpart to the README. From there, you can navigate within a page by headings, using the H key in browse mode for NVDA or JAWS. Internal links between chapters and appendices should work, so cross-references take you directly where you need to go. And it's worth bookmarking that URL so you can get back to it quickly during the workshop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This sounds especially good for accessibility.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The HTML pages include skip links, breadcrumb navigation, and ARIA landmarks. So for screen reader users, jumping to the main landmark with D in NVDA or R in JAWS can make navigation much faster. And if you want the deeper technical background on how GitHub Pages publishing works, there's a separate appendix dedicated to GitHub Pages.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's say I don't want a separate website. I just want to read directly on GitHub.com.

speaker: ALEX
text: You can do that too. Go to the repository page your facilitator shares. On GitHub.com, the README renders automatically on the main repository page, so you begin with a formatted overview. Then click into the docs folder to find all the chapters and appendices. When you open any Markdown file ending in dot md, GitHub renders it as formatted text with headings, links, and code blocks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds straightforward visually, but what about screen reader navigation inside the repository?

speaker: ALEX
text: A few important details. The repository file listing behaves like a grid or table. You can use T to jump to the file table, then move through rows with arrow keys. Once you're inside a file, GitHub renders the Markdown, so heading navigation with H works well. At the top of a file view, breadcrumb links show the path, for example repository, then docs, then a specific file. Those breadcrumbs help you move back up a level. And again, on the repository main page, T opens the Go to File finder so you can type the filename directly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's one of those shortcuts that can save a lot of effort.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if I want everything on my own machine?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then you're in the downloading section, and there are two main approaches. Option A is cloning with Git, which is the recommended method. Option B is downloading a ZIP if you don't want to use Git.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the Git option first.

speaker: ALEX
text: Cloning gives you a full copy of the repository and lets you update later with git pull. The commands are straightforward: first run git clone with the repository URL, then change into the folder with cd Learning-Room.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And after that, what do I actually have?

speaker: ALEX
text: You have the whole repository on your computer: Markdown source, HTML output, scripts, and learning-room materials. If you're using a screen reader, it's a good moment to open the folder in VS Code, for example with code dot, then use Control+Shift+E for the Explorer panel. From there, arrow through files and press Enter to open one in the editor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So clone is best if I want a living copy, not just a one-time download.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now the no-Git route: ZIP download.

speaker: ALEX
text: If Git isn't installed, or you simply prefer not to use the command line, go to the repository page on GitHub.com. Find the green Code button near the top of the file listing. Activate it to open the dropdown menu, then choose Download ZIP. Save the file and extract it on your computer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for screen reader users, that Code button can be annoying to find if you don't know where it lives.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right, so the appendix gives very specific guidance. The Code button is near the repository description and after the branch selector. In NVDA or JAWS browse mode, press B to jump between buttons until you hear Code. Activate it with Enter or Space. Then arrow down in the dropdown until you reach Download ZIP.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Important caveat?

speaker: ALEX
text: A ZIP download is a snapshot. It does not update automatically. If the repository changes, your ZIP copy does not know that. You'll need to download a fresh ZIP later if you want a newer version.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Sometimes people don't need everything. What if I only want one chapter or one cheat sheet?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then download an individual file. From GitHub.com, navigate to the file, activate the Raw button above the file content, and the browser will show the raw text of the file. Then save it with Control+S on Windows or Linux, or Command+S on macOS.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if it's an HTML file instead of Markdown?

speaker: ALEX
text: Go to the html folder, find the corresponding dot html file, and use the same Raw then Save approach.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if I've already cloned the repository?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then you already have every file locally. Just open the folder and copy out the specific files you need.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about folder structure, because that can be confusing when learners first open a repository.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. The docs folder contains all 17 numbered chapters, from 00 through 16, plus 31 appendices labeled A through Z and then AA through AE. Those are Markdown files.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So docs is the source reading material in Markdown.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. The html folder contains pre-built HTML versions of every Markdown file. Within that, html slash docs contains HTML versions of all chapters and appendices specifically. The learning-room folder contains practice repository files, challenges, group exercises, and setup guides, again mainly in Markdown. The html slash learning-room folder contains HTML versions of those learning-room documents.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there are the project-maintenance folders.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The dot github folder includes issue templates, a pull request template, Copilot agents, and slash commands, usually in YAML and Markdown. The scripts folder contains the build script that converts Markdown to HTML, using JavaScript. And the repository root contains items like the README, agendas, facilitator guide, contributing guide, and other project files.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's useful, because it tells learners not just where to read, but where different kinds of work happen.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly right.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now suppose I'm offline. Can I still use all this effectively?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Once you've downloaded or cloned the repository, you can read everything offline.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's separate Markdown and HTML.

speaker: ALEX
text: For Markdown files, open the dot md files in any text editor. They are plain text with lightweight markup. In VS Code, Control+Shift+V opens a rendered Markdown preview, which many learners find easier to read than raw syntax.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the HTML path?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open html slash index dot html in any web browser. From there, internal links between pages should work locally, so you can move through the material without internet access. You can navigate among chapters using the links on the pages themselves, including bottom navigation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Accessibility still works offline?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The HTML files work offline the same way they do online. The ARIA landmarks, heading structure, and skip links are built into each file.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If someone asks for the best offline setup, what would you recommend?

speaker: ALEX
text: Two strong options. One is VS Code with the Markdown files: open the repository folder, browse with the Explorer, and read source files directly, using the preview when needed. The other is a browser with the local HTML site: open html slash index dot html and navigate from there. That gives the most polished reading experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, now the maintenance question. How do I keep my copy updated?

speaker: ALEX
text: If you cloned with Git, navigate to your local copy, typically with cd Learning-Room, and run git pull.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if I haven't changed anything locally, that should just update cleanly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you have not made local changes, git pull should update your copy cleanly. If you have made local edits, Git will attempt to merge those changes with the incoming updates. And if that creates conflicts, you'll need merge conflict skills from the chapter on merge conflicts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also one more step mentioned here after pulling.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. After pulling, rebuild the HTML so your local generated site matches the latest Markdown source. The command given is npm run build:html.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if I used a ZIP instead of a clone?

speaker: ALEX
text: Then there is no incremental update mechanism. You simply download a fresh ZIP from the repository and replace your local copy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This brings us to the big decision question: which format should I use?

speaker: ALEX
text: If you're reading during the workshop, GitHub Pages, meaning the online HTML site, is usually the best choice. It requires no setup, stays current, and is designed to be fully accessible.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For offline reference?

speaker: ALEX
text: Local HTML from the html folder. It works in any browser, doesn't require internet, and gives you the styled reading experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For editing and contributing?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the Markdown source files in docs. Those are the canonical source files you edit, and then the HTML is rebuilt from them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For a quick lookup of one section while I'm already in a browser?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub.com is great for that. You can jump straight to a file without downloading everything.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For archiving a copy?

speaker: ALEX
text: Either a ZIP download or a Git clone gives you a full snapshot. But for staying updated long-term, a Git clone is better because you can use one command, git pull.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's revisit the learning-card guidance by audience, because it's full of useful detail.

speaker: ALEX
text: For screen reader users, the GitHub Pages HTML site is usually the most accessible choice during the workshop because every page includes skip links, landmarks, and consistent heading structure. For offline reference, the local HTML files in the html folder work essentially the same way. For contributing edits, work in the Markdown source in docs using VS Code, because that's the canonical source that generates everything else.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And low vision guidance?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Pages and local HTML both support browser zoom to 200 percent or more without layout breakage, so choose whichever gives you the better reading experience. VS Code's Markdown preview inherits your editor theme, which is helpful if you use a high-contrast theme. And if you're reading on GitHub.com, GitHub's dark theme can reduce eye strain during long sessions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for sighted users broadly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the GitHub Pages site during the workshop for the cleanest hyperlinked reading experience with no setup. Clone the repository if you want a permanent offline copy, and remember that html slash index dot html acts as a local homepage. And if you want to improve the materials, edit the Markdown in docs; those changes are what get rebuilt into HTML, typically through the project's build process and automation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap up, I want to make sure we keep the source-of-truth piece in view. Where should learners verify current facts if GitHub changes something?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the official references: GitHub Docs and the GitHub Changelog. Those are the authoritative external sources listed for this appendix. The appendix also provides a section-level source map, pointing each major part of this topic back to those official references.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the practical takeaway is: choose the format that matches your goal.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If you want easiest reading during the workshop, use GitHub Pages. If you want quick browser access, use GitHub.com. If you want a full offline, updateable copy, clone the repository. If you want a one-time snapshot with no Git required, download a ZIP. And if you're contributing, edit the Markdown source.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a helpful framework, especially for learners who may have assumed there was only one correct way to access the materials.

speaker: ALEX
text: There isn't one single correct way. There is a best-fit way for your task, your device, and your accessibility needs.

speaker: ALEX
text: And from here, learners can continue on to the appendix about GitHub Skills, revisit the resources appendix, or return to the pre-workshop setup material that this appendix supports.

speaker: JAMIE
text: See you next time.
